method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The modern Icelandic name of the country is Ísland, usually rendered in English as Iceland.[2][4][5] The name is traditionally explained as an Old Norse compound meaning “ice land,” and it is generally associated with the Viking age rather than with Celtic naming traditions.[2][4] According to the saga tradition preserved in *Íslendingabók* and *Landnámabók*, several early Norse explorers were said to have named the island, including Naddodd, who called it Snæland (“Snow Land”), and Garðar Svavarsson, who called it Garðarshólmur (“Garðar’s Isle”).[2][4] The name Ísland is usually credited to Hrafna-Flóki Vilgerðarson, whose account of the island’s icy conditions is said to have given rise to the lasting form of the name.[4] By the time Greenland was named, Iceland had already been called Ísland for generations.[4] In Icelandic, the language itself is closely related to Old Norse, and the country’s place-name tradition reflects the long continuity of the language from the Viking age to the present.[5]

==History==
Iceland was settled in the late 9th century by Norse migrants, led in the traditional account by Ingólfur Arnarson, and later became closely tied to the wider Norse world through further exploration toward Greenland and Vinland.[3] Earlier contact may have occurred through Irish-Scottish monks who are thought to have visited the island in the 8th century, though permanent settlement began only with the arrival of the Norse.[4]

The island’s history has been deeply shaped by its geology, and Iceland possesses an exceptionally well documented record of volcanic activity extending over the past 11 centuries.[2] Written accounts from the 12th and 13th centuries, combined with preserved lava flows, vent structures, tephra layers, and chronostratigraphic field studies, have made it possible to reconstruct historical eruptions with unusual precision.[2] This record includes 205 historical eruptive events, ranging from small eruptions to long-lived fissure eruptions that produced between 0.01 and 20 cubic kilometres of magma.[2]

Among the most significant volcanic events in Icelandic history were the Eldgjá eruption of 934–940 and the Laki eruption of 1783–1784, both in South Iceland.[2] These were the two largest volcanic events in Iceland’s history and among the largest of their kind on Earth in the last 2,000 years.[2] Iceland’s long geological development is linked to the activity of the Iceland mantle plume, which has helped form the North Atlantic Igneous Province and continues to sustain the island’s active volcanic zones.[2]

Earthquakes are frequent in Iceland, but they rarely cause serious damage, in part because modern buildings are generally constructed to withstand severe shocks.[3] The island’s volcanic and geothermal features, including numerous hot springs and solfataras, have also remained prominent elements of its historical development and identity.[3]

==Geography==
Iceland is a volcanic island in the North Atlantic Ocean, situated just south of the Arctic Circle between Greenland Sea and the North Atlantic, with Reykjavík as its capital and the country’s northernmost capital city. [1][2][5]  
It lies on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where the Eurasian and North American plates diverge, and its geology is strongly influenced by a hotspot and deep mantle plume that drive frequent volcanic and geothermal activity. [1][3][5]  
The island’s landscape is characterized by lava fields, volcanoes, glaciers, waterfalls, black sand beaches, fjords, rugged coastlines, plateaus, mountains, and fertile lowlands. [1][2][4][5]  
Iceland is often described as a land of fire and ice because volcanic eruptions and glaciers are both prominent features of the environment. [2][4][5]  
Glaciers cover about 10 to 11 percent of the country, with the largest ice caps—Vatnajökull, Langjökull, and Hofsjökull—located in the central highlands. [2][3][5]  
Large parts of Iceland are sparsely inhabited, and roughly half of the country is volcanic desert, much of it in the uninhabited Central Highlands. [1]  
Only a small share of the land is suitable for agriculture, with pasture concentrated near coastal population centers and cultivation limited to about 1 percent of the island. [1]  
The country’s location and geology also make it well suited to geothermal power, with numerous high-temperature and low-temperature geothermal sites across the island. [3]

==Politics==
Iceland is a constitutional republic with a multi-party system, and its parliament, the Althingi, is one of the oldest legislative assemblies in the world.[3][4] Executive power is exercised by the government, while the president serves as head of state and is elected by direct popular vote for a four-year term.[3] The Althingi is a unicameral legislature with 63 members elected every four years, and the president عادة invites the leader best able to command parliamentary support to form a cabinet.[3][4] Cabinet ministers normally remain in office until the next general election or the formation of a new government, and they must retain majority support in parliament or avoid a vote of censure.[3][4] The judiciary is independent of the executive and legislature, and Iceland’s courts are headed by the Supreme Court.[3][4] 

The present constitutional framework was established in 1944, when Iceland declared independence after centuries under Norwegian and Danish rule.[1][4] Although politics before independence were largely shaped by the struggle for self-determination, contemporary politics has been strongly influenced by the 2008 financial crisis and its aftermath.[1] Iceland’s party system has also been reshaped in recent years, with new parties gaining prominence and coalition alignments changing, while rural-urban and left-right divisions remain important political cleavages.[1] The president is largely a ceremonial figure, although the office can serve as a politically neutral platform for public debate, as illustrated by Halla Tómasdóttir’s election in 2024.[2]

==Economy==
Iceland has a small but advanced mixed economy that combines free-market capitalism with a substantial welfare state.[1][2] The economy has long relied on fishing, tourism, and aluminum smelting, with fishing still providing a major share of export earnings and remaining vulnerable to fish-stock fluctuations and global price changes.[1][2] Since 2010, tourism has become one of the main engines of growth, and the sector accounted for a large share of goods-and-services exports in 2024.[1][2]  

The country’s limited domestic market has encouraged diversification into manufacturing and services, including software production, biotechnology, and knowledge-intensive activities.[1][4] Abundant geothermal and hydropower resources have also supported investment in aluminum processing and attracted interest from high-tech firms seeking access to low-cost renewable energy.[1] Manufacturing remains relatively modest as a share of employment, while service industries make up a large part of the workforce.[4]  

Iceland experienced strong post-pandemic recovery after a sharp contraction in 2020, followed by robust GDP growth in 2022 and 2023 before a slight decline in 2024.[2] Inflation rose significantly in the early 2020s but eased by 2025, while unemployment remained low and the labor market continued to depend partly on foreign workers for some jobs.[2] Tourism has recovered strongly after the COVID-19 downturn, with visitor numbers rising again in 2024 and projected to increase further in 2025.[2]

==Demographics==
Iceland has a population of about 402,329 in 2026, with an annual growth rate of 1.02%.[1] The country remains sparsely populated by global standards, and its population has continued to rise in recent years.[1][5]  

The median age in Iceland is 36.6 years, reflecting a relatively mature population structure.[1] Iceland’s population is generally described as having moved into the fourth stage of the demographic transition, characterized by low fertility, low mortality, and high life expectancy.[5]  

In early 2024, just over half of Iceland’s population was male, while females accounted for about 48%; a small share identified as non-binary or with other genders.[3] Around 80% of the population was Icelandic and about 20% had foreign backgrounds, reflecting increased immigration in recent decades.[3]  

Immigration has played an important role in Iceland’s recent population growth, especially since 2005, when labor demand and economic expansion attracted foreign workers.[5] By February 2024, immigrants and people with foreign backgrounds made up roughly one-fifth of the population, with Poles forming the largest immigrant group.[3]  

Iceland’s population has grown from 50,358 in the first census of 1703 to more than 383,000 at the beginning of 2024, and to 387,558 in 2023 according to WHO data.[4][5] Historical population change was shaped by epidemics, crop failures, volcanic eruptions, and periods of extreme cold, which often caused deaths to exceed births.[5]  

Life expectancy in Iceland is high, and the country also tracks age distribution and healthy life expectancy as part of its demographic profile.[4]

==Culture==
The Icelandic government supports the preservation of historical sites and cultural artefacts as part of an effort to pass the national cultural heritage on to future generations, and it is also responsible for museums and national cultural institutions.[1] Through these institutions, the state supports independent research in Icelandic history and culture and provides the public with opportunities to learn about the country’s past.[1] The Ministry of Culture and Business Affairs is responsible for the arts and general cultural affairs, and state support is intended to encourage artistic variety, creativity, and initiative while ensuring access to arts and culture for all residents regardless of social status.[1] Government involvement in the arts is focused mainly on state-run artistic institutions and support for professional artists.[1]  

The ministry’s responsibilities also extend to youth affairs outside formal education, including support for youth organisations and research in the field, which it has carried out since 1992.[1] Sports fall under the same ministry, which gathers data on sporting activity and facilities and oversees anti-doping programmes.[1] The media, including the Media Commission and the Icelandic National Broadcasting Service (RÚV), are also under the ministry’s auspices.[1]  

Icelandic cultural life has often been shaped by a strong sense of national heritage, with the sagas and traditional celebrations used historically to reinforce continuity with the past.[2] At the same time, contemporary Icelandic culture is increasingly international, especially as most Icelanders now live in the capital area.[2] The country is also noted for an egalitarian social ethos and quiet, reserved public comportment.[2]  

Folklore remains an important part of Icelandic culture, including beliefs and stories about elves, trolls, sea monsters, land spirits, ghosts, and the Yule Cat.[3][5] Seasonal observances and traditional feasts also remain significant, such as Þorrablót, during which Icelanders gather to eat traditional foods, sing, play games, and tell stories.[4] Iceland is likewise known for its vibrant music and arts scene, which is often mentioned alongside its natural landmarks and cultural attractions.[4]
